formulas.name: Law of Diminishing Returns
formulas.statement: As the quantity of a variable factor is increased, beyond a certain point, the marginal output will eventually decrease.
formulas.notes: This concept is discussed in the context of economic systems in the chapter

formulas.name: Marginal Utility
formulas.statement: The additional satisfaction or utility derived from consuming one more unit of a product or service.
formulas.notes: This concept is used to explain the law of diminishing returns

formulas.name: Diminishing Marginal Utility
formulas.statement: The marginal utility derived from each additional unit of a product or service decreases as consumption increases.
formulas.notes: This concept is discussed in the context of consumer behavior in the chapter

formulas.name: Opportunity Cost
formulas.statement: The value of the next best alternative foregone when a choice is made.
formulas.notes: This concept is used to explain the trade-offs involved in decision-making

formulas.name: Scarcity
formulas.statement: The fundamental economic problem of having unlimited wants but limited resources.
formulas.notes: This concept is discussed in the context of economic systems in the chapter

formulas.name: Production Possibilities Curve
formulas.statement: A graph that shows the various combinations of two goods that can be produced with given resources and technology.
formulas.notes: This concept is used to illustrate the trade-offs involved in production decisions

formulas.name: Consumer Surplus
formulas.statement: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay for a product or service and what they actually pay.
formulas.notes: This concept is used to explain the benefits of trade
